coredata.dc:title: Facile synthesis of fluorescent sulfur quantum dots for selective detection of p-nitrophenol in water samples
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Xiaohui
Wang, Ya
Luo, Zhen
Zhang, Baowen
Mei, Xiaolin
Yang, Xiupei
coredata.dc:description: P-nitrophenol (p-NP) is widely present in soil and water environment, which brings great harm to the ecosystem. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a rapid, sensitive, and selective method for detecting p-NP. In this work, fluorescent sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) were synthesized using a simple top-down approach. The prepared SQDs are monodisperse, uniformly distributed, and the size is about 2.9 nm. After adding p-NP, due to the internal filtering effect (IFE) between p-NP and SQDs, the fluorescence of SQDs can be effectively quenched. It is worth noting that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the SQDs sensor shows an excellent linear relationship when the concentration of p-NP is 0.2–30 µM and 30–90 µM, respectively. The detection limit is 70 nM, which is better than other reported fluorescence sensing methods. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the detection of p-NP in water samples. Excellent recoveries of 90.9 ~ 102.0% and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 0.33 ~ 1.66% were obtained, respectively, which confirmed the great potential of SQDs in the actual measurement of p-NP in environmental samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur quantum dots
P-nitrophenol
Fluorescence quenching
Internal filtering effect

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of SQDs for p-NP detection
Sensing p-NP in the environmental water sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) AFM image, (B) three-dimensional particle size distribution of AFM, (C) FTIR spectrum of SQDs. (D) High resolution XPS analysis of S 2p.

(A) Normalized Uv-vis absorption spectra (blue curve), excitation (black curve) and emission (red curve) spectra of SQDs. (B) PL spectra of SQDs under different excitation wavelength spectra from 280 to 400 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Spectra of fluorescence-scattered light system in the existence of different concentrations of p-NP under excitation of 360 nm. (B) Fluorescence response of SQDs with the p-NP concentration increased. Linear relationships between F0/F and p-NP level in the range of 0.2–30 μM (C) and 30–90 μM (D), respectively.

(A) The Stern-Volmer equation at different temperatures. (B) Time-resolved fluorescence-decay curve of SQDs before (up) and after (down) addition of p-NP (λ ex = 360 nm). (C) Uv-vis absorption of SQDs with the addition of different concentrations of p-NP. (D) Normalized Uv-vis absorption spectra (blue curve) of p-NP, excitation (black curve) and emission (red curve) spectra of SQDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Scheme of the synthetic strategy for SQDs and the principle of p-NP sensing.

head.ce:title: Facile synthesis of fluorescent sulfur quantum dots for selective detection of p-nitrophenol in water samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chemical Synthesis and Pollution Control Key Laboratory of Sichuan Province, China West Normal University, Nanchong 637000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: P-nitrophenol (p-NP) is widely present in soil and water environment, which brings great harm to the ecosystem. Therefore, it is of great significance to establish a rapid, sensitive, and selective method for detecting p-NP. In this work, fluorescent sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) were synthesized using a simple top-down approach. The prepared SQDs are monodisperse, uniformly distributed, and the size is about 2.9 nm. After adding p-NP, due to the internal filtering effect (IFE) between p-NP and SQDs, the fluorescence of SQDs can be effectively quenched. It is worth noting that the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the SQDs sensor shows an excellent linear relationship when the concentration of p-NP is 0.2–30 µM and 30–90 µM, respectively. The detection limit is 70 nM, which is better than other reported fluorescence sensing methods. The proposed method has been successfully applied to the detection of p-NP in water samples. Excellent recoveries of 90.9 ~ 102.0% and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 0.33 ~ 1.66% were obtained, respectively, which confirmed the great potential of SQDs in the actual measurement of p-NP in environmental samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrophenol compounds are explosives, organic compounds containing one or more nitro functional groups (−NO2), including m-nitrophenol(m-NP), o-nitrophenol (o-NP), p-nitrophenol (p-NP), 2,6-dinitrophenol (2,6-DNP), 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP), etc. [1–3]. The residues of nitrophenol compounds in the environment are highly stable and toxic, which seriously damage the ecological environment and seriously threaten human health because they can strongly stimulate the skin, eyes, and human organs [4,5]. As we all know, the three isomers of nitrophenol are o-nitrophenol (o-NP), m-nitrophenol (m-NP), and p-nitrophenol (p-NP), which exhibit different toxicity. Therefore, the identification and selective detection of each nitrophenol isomer is very important for human health and environmental protection [6,7]. In particular, p-NP is a toxic hydrolysate of the insecticide parathion and paraoxon. It has been detected not only in freshwater but also in industrial wastewater and marine environments [7–10]. Many methods for detecting p-NP have been reported, such as electrochemical [11,12], capillary electrophoresis detection [13,14], chromatographic [15]. Nevertheless, these methods are accompanied by many disadvantages, such as the need for complex and expensive equipment, unstable electrodes, and complicated pretreatment procedures. They are not suitable for rapid monitoring. On the contrary, fluorescence detection [16–20] is an analytical method with low cost, high sensitivity, and a simple sample preparation procedure. Therefore, it can meet the requirements of rapid, sensitives and selective detection of p-NP in environmental monitoring.

In recent years, exploring high-performance fluorescent materials to construct various chemical sensing methods has become a research hotspot in the field of analysis. Due to the three-dimensional quantum limitation, quantum dots (QDs) exhibit different electronic and optical properties from traditional fluorescent molecules (organic dyes and fluorescent proteins) [21]. With the in-depth research on QDs, people pay more attention to those that do not contain heavy metals, such as phosphorus [22,23], carbon [24,25], sulfur [26–30], and silicon QDs [31–34]. These non-metal-containing QDs have the advantages of low toxicity, stable photoluminescence, and good solubility. Compared with carbon, phosphorus, and silicon, sulfur has bactericidal and anti-tumor effects [35–38]. Sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) have attracted much attention because of their good biocompatibility, low toxicity, convenient preparation, and environmental friendliness. In 2014, a pioneering work about luminescent S-dots had been reported by Li’s group, in which the S-dots were synthesized by a phase interfacial reaction [39]. Subsequently, Shen's research group innovatively proposed a top-down method, using polyethylene glycol-400 (PEG-400) to treat sublimated sulfur powder under alkaline conditions to synthesize sulfur spots with adjustable emission [40]. Wang and co-workers found that H2O2 treatment can significantly increase the fluorescence quantum yield of SQDs [27]. In addition, they also developed the use of ultrasound to promote the synthesis of SQDs, shortening the reaction time to less than 5 h [41]. In 2019, Wang's research group reported on the “switch” of sulfur quantum dots for the fluorescence detection of cobalt ions and norfloxacin [26]. Sun's research team used the hydrothermal method to shorten the reaction time by increasing the temperature and pressure of the system and successfully synthesized green luminescent SQDs [42]. Qu and co-workers reported a top-down microwave-assisted method that produced a cyan emission SQDs with a fluorescence quantum yield close to 50% [43]. In 2020, Zhou's research group used the reaction in an oxygen atmosphere to obtain monodisperse SQDs that emit stable fluorescence in the blue-green region, with a fluorescence quantum yield of 21.5% [44]. In addition, the application of carboxymethyl cellulose SQDs in the sensitive detection of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid and live-cell imaging has also been reported [45]. Recently, Wang's research group reported that SQDs were modified on gold electrodes, and silver ions could be selectively detected by electrochemical methods [27].

In this work, we synthesized SQDs using a one-pot method, which has simple synthesis steps and few by-products. The synthesized SQDs have excitation-dependent characteristics. When excited by 360 nm, the SQDs solution emits strong blue fluorescence at 440 nm. In addition to the high stability of the SQDs in a wide pH range, they also have excellent water dispersibility and long-term stability. The synthesized SQDs can be directly used as a highly selective, fast, and sensitive p-NP detection sensor without any functional modification (Scheme 1 ). This sensor has been successfully applied to the detection of p-NP in actual water samples (tap water, river water, and mineral water). The results show that the proposed method can be used as an alternative method for the determination of p-NP in environmental samples.

All chemicals involved were commercially available and of analytical grade without any additional purification. Calcium chloride (CaCl2, 99%), barium chloride dihydrate (BaCl2, 99%), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, 99%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 98%), chromium chloride hexahydrate (CrCl3·6H2O, 99%), cadmium chloride hydrate (CdCl2, 99%), ferric(III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, 99%), aluminum trichloride (AlCl3, 99%), cupric chloride anhydrous (CuCl2, 99%), zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 99%), manganese(II) chloride (MnCl2, 99%), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99%), cobalt chloride (CoCl2, 99%), polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG-400), sodium bromide (NaBr, 99%), sodium phosphate (Na3PO4, 99%), sodium fluoride (NaF, 99%), sodium nitrate (NaNO3, 99%), sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, 99%), sodium nitrite (NaNO2, 99%), sodium sulfate (Na2SO4, 99%), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, 99%), hydroquinone (HQ), catechol, resorcinol, phenol, m-nitrophenol (m-NP), o-nitrophenol (o-NP), p-nitrophenol (p-NP), were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Corp. (Shanghai, China). Sublimed sulfur (S, 99%) was purchased from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China).

The infrared spectra were obtained on a Nicolet 6700 spectrometer (Thermo Electron Corp., Madison, WI, USA). The surface elemental composition was analyzed by a Thermo Scientific™ K-Alpha™+ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Thermo Fisher Scientific Corp., Ltd., Waltham, MA, USA). Surface morphologies of the SQDs were imaged using an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) (FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) and a JPK atomic force microscope (AFM) (Bruker Nano GmbH, Berlin, Germany), respectively. All ultraviolet-visible (UV-Vis) absorption spectra were conducted with a UV-2550 UV-vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan). Fluorescence spectra were taken on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Palo Alto, CA, USA). The fluorescence lifetime was estimated by an FLS980 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Inc., Livingston, Scotland, UK).

According to the method reported in the previous studies [40], SQDs are synthesized by a slightly modified top-down method. In short, PEG-400 (3.0 mL), sublimed sulfur powder (1.4 g), ultrapure water (60 mL), and sodium hydroxide (4.0 g) were added to a 100 mL round bottom flask, respectively. The mixture was continuously stirred for 72 h in an oil bath at 70 °C. After half an hour, it could be observed that the sublimed sulfur powder gradually dissolves, and the solution turned reddish-brown. With the extension of heating time, the mixture turned pale yellow. Afterward, dialysis was performed with a membrane of 500–1000 Da. The membrane was activated with slightly boiling pure water for half an hour and washed with ultrapure water. The resulting mixture solution was then introduced for 48 h, and the water outside the membrane was replaced every 8 h to ensure no osmotic equilibrium so that small molecules were output in the process. Finally, the dialyzed SQDs products were stored in a refrigerator (4 °C) for subsequent experiments.

Firstly, 600 µL of SQDs aqueous dispersion (2 mg/mL), 600 µL of phosphate buffer solution (0.1 M), and a known concentration of p-NP standard solution were added to a 5.0 mL centrifuge tube, and then diluted the solution to 3.0 mL with ultrapure water. The mixed solution was shaken and mixed at room temperature for 5 min, and finally excited with 360 nm excitation light, and the fluorescence intensity emitted at 440 nm was measured.

The actual environmental water samples involved in this study were: river water samples were taken from the Jialing River in Sichuan, tap water and mineral water samples were taken from the campus of China West Normal University. These collected water samples were all used 0.22 μm membrane filters to remove suspended particles before analysis. The following detection procedures are similar to those for the detection of p-NP in the phosphate buffer above.

They were using polyethylene glycol and sublimed sulfur powder as a stabilizer and raw materials, respectively. SQDs with good water solubility and high stability were synthesized by a one-pot method under alkaline conditions. The distribution and morphology of the synthesized SQDs were characterized by AFM (Fig. 1 A and 1B) and TEM (Fig. S1A and S1B), respectively. As shown in Fig. 1A and 1B, the AFM images show that the prepared SQDs were monodisperse and uniformly distributed, with a narrow distribution in the size of about 2.9 nm. The illustration in Fig. S1A and S1B also shows that the size distribution of SQDs was relatively uniform and the average diameter of SQDs is 3.7 ± 1.5 nm, which was consistent with the characterization results of AFM.

Fig. 1C shows the FTIR of S, PEG-400 and SQDs, further confirming the group composition and structural characteristics of SQDs. The peaks at 3430 cm−1 and 1630 cm−1 belong to the stretching vibration and the bending vibration of −OH, respectively. In contrast, the peak at 2930 cm−1 belongs to the stretching vibration of the saturated C–H bond. The peaks at 1460 and 1360 cm−1 are the bending vibrations of the C–H bond in PEG-400, which belong to the characteristic absorption peaks of PEG-400. The peak at 1240 cm−1 is attributed to the C–O–H stretching vibration. The absorption peaks at 1000 and 1120 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration of C–O–C and C–O–H. In most cases, due to the low intensity and poor resolution of the fingerprint area of the functional group, the vibration signal is often blurred, but there is still a prominent signal peak in the FTIR spectrum of the SQDs. The peak at 680 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of S-O, and the characteristic absorption peak at 520 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of S-S [45,46]. The infrared spectrum of SQDs showed no other new peaks. It is almost the same as the characteristic peaks of the PEG-400 infrared spectrum, indicating that there is no chemical reaction between SQDs and PEG, and PEG is physically adsorbed on the surface of SQDs to prevent sample aggregation.

According to the results of infrared spectroscopy, XPS spectra further proved the successful synthesis of SQDs. XPS spectrum result shows that SQDs are mainly composed of C, O, and S elements (Fig. S1C). The amount of sulfur in the XPS survey spectra is very low compared to that of carbon and oxygen. Because the detection depth of XPS is not very large and can only detect the content of elements on the surface of the material. There may be a large number of PEG-400 groups on the surface of the synthesized SQDs, resulting in low S content in the XPS scan results. This result is consistent with many other literature reports [26,41]. The high-resolution XPS S2p spectrum contains four different peaks (Fig. 1D), all of which belong to the sulfur element, located at 162.7, 164.0, 166.2, and 168.3 eV. The peaks of elemental sulfur at 162.7 and 164.0 eV, and the binding energies at 166.2 and 168.3 eV were composed of SO4 2−(2p2/3), SO3 2−(2p1/2), and SO3 2−(2p2/3), respectively. The above analysis results show that SQDs are mainly composed of elemental sulfur, abundant sulfonate, and surface sulfonate groups, similar to the previously reported SQDs [27,47].

Under the irradiation of 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, SQDs aqueous solution could emit bright blue fluorescence. In order to further study their optical properties, we systematically investigated their absorption, excitation, and emission spectra. As shown in Fig. 2A, a strong peak near 213 nm can be observed, which might be caused by the n → σ* transition. A new absorption peak around 266 nm might be caused by the n → π* transition of S atom. At the same time, a peak around 326 nm can be observed, caused by the formation of SQDs. Like the previously reported SQDs [45], they also exhibit the characteristic that the emission wavelength depends on the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2 B). As the excitation wavelength increases from 280 to 400 nm, the corresponding emission wavelength is redshifted from 435 to 493 nm. The maximum emission peak appears at 440 nm, and the corresponding excitation wavelength is 360 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of SQDs reached 1.08 % under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. S2A, the fluorescence intensity of SQDs does not change in the pH range of 5–12. Here, F is the fluorescence intensity of SQDs at different pH values, and F0 is the fluorescence intensity at pH = 5. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of SQDs could decrease with increasing temperature but could be restored after cooling. The fluorescence intensity didn’t change after five cycles, indicating that the stability of SQDs is good (Fig. S2B). The possible reasons for the fluorescence intensity remaining basically unchanged after repeated cycles between 15 and 55 °C are as follows: with the increase of solution temperature, the viscosity of the medium decreases, increasing the chance of fluorescence molecule and solvent molecule collision quenching, leading to the decrease of fluorescence intensity; on the contrary, the fluorescence intensity gradually increases with the decrease of temperature [48].

SQDs have low toxicity, good water solubility, good biocompatibility, and rich surface groups, which have broad application prospects in chemistry and biology. It was worth noting that the fluorescence intensity of SQDs decreases continuously with the increase of p-NP concentration. Therefore, this satisfactory result prompted us to establish a sensing platform for detecting p-NP. In order to make SQDs have a better fluorescence response to p-NP, we investigated the conditions such as pH value, reaction time, temperature and SQDs concentration. The fluorescence quenching efficiency (F0/F) of the system increases gradually from pH 3.0 to 8.0 and reaches the maximum at pH 8.0.Further increase in pH results in a decrease in F0/F. Therefore, pH = 8.0 is selected as the best detection condition (Fig. S3A). After adding a certain concentration of p-NP, the fluorescence quenching effect gradually increased within five minutes and then tended to be stable, indicating that the reaction between p-NP and SQDS was very fast and the static complex could be formed within 5 min (Fig. S3B). The reaction temperature is an important factor in determining the fluorescence measurement. When the temperature is too high, the fluorescence intensity of SQDs itself will decrease. The final choice is to detect p-NP at 25 °C (Fig. S3C).When the concentration is too low, the number of fluorescence molecules is limited, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency is low.When the concentration is too high, the fluorescence quenching efficiency decreases due to the self-quenching effect.When the probe concentration is 0.40 mg/mL, the fluorescence quenching efficiency is the highest, so 0.4 mg/mL is selected the optimal detection conditions (Fig. S3D) [49].

Under optimal conditions, the sensitivity of the proposed method for detecting p-NP was calculated by recording the changes in the fluorescence intensity of the SQDs probe at 440 nm. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when the concentration of p-N increases, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases in the range of 0.2 to 90 μM, accompanied by a significant red shift of fluorescence emission. As the concentration of p-NP increases, the red shift of the emission peak may be due to the closer combination between SQDs and p-NP through hydrogen bond interaction, which is also consistent with the static quenching mechanism described Fig. 3B, 3C and 3D show that the fluorescence quenching efficiency has two good linear relationships against the p-NP concentration in the range of 0.2–30 μM and 30–90 μM. The regression equation is F0/F = 0.0261C + 1.01022 (R2 = 0.999) and F0/F = 0.05228C + 0.12119 (R2 = 0.995). F and F0 represent the fluorescence intensity of SQDs with and without p-NP, and C is the concentration of p-NP, respectively. According to 3σ/slope (where σ is the standard deviation), the detection limit of p-NP was 70 nM. Similar to previous reports, as the concentration of the target analyte increases, its quenching of the fluorescence signal exhibits two different linear segments [50,51], but a clear explanation is still lacking, and further exploration is needed. We compared this method's linear range and LOD with the reported methods for detecting p-nitrophenol (Table S1). Fortunately, although the sensitivity of the proposed method is not the highest among these methods, its detection limit can fully meet the actual analysis needs. It can be predicted that this method does not involve complicated sample pre-processing procedures and has great potential for rapid detection of p-NP in actual environmental samples.

In order to confirm the selectivity of p-NP to SQD fluorescence quenching, the interference of various environmentally relevant anions (SO4 2−, SO3 2−, H2PO4 −, PO4 3−, HPO4 2−, IO3 −, Ac−, Br−, Cl−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 −, F−, S2−, and ClO−), metal ions and small organic molecules (Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Na+, o-DHB, m-DHB, PHE, BA, and HQ) on the determination of p-NP have been investigated. The concentration of Fe2+, Fe3+, o-NP and m-NP is 30 µM, and that of other interferences is 700 μM. These interfering ions basically do not increase or decrease the fluorescence intensity of SQDs. p-NP can significantly quench the fluorescence intensity of SQDs. In contrast, the fluorescence quenching effect of other ions on SQDs can be ignored or slightly affected (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of SQDs for p-NP detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence quenching mechanisms of SQDs are diverse, including static quenching, dynamic quenching, light-induced electron transfer, fluorescence resonance energy transfer, internal filtering effect (IFE), and surface energy transfer. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the Stern-Volmer plot is a straight line, and the slope gradually decreases with the increase of temperature, indicating that it conforms to the static quenching type. Measure the SQDs separately, add the fluorescence lifetime of 20 µM and 60 µM p-NP SQDs solutions, respectively. It can be found from Fig. 4B that the change in fluorescence lifetime can be ignored (τ1 = 1.83 ns, τ2 = 1.75 ns, τ3 = 1.70 ns), which further confirms the mechanism of static quenching. As shown in Fig. 4C, with the addition of p-NP, a new characteristic absorption peak appears at 400 nm, and as the concentration of p-NP increases, the absorbance also increases. Such experimental results show that non-fluorescent compounds or complexes was formed between the SQDs and p-NP, which belongs to static fluorescence quenching, and there is no fluorescence resonance energy transfer phenomenon. As shown in Fig. 4D, the absorption band of p-NP overlaps with the excitation band of SQDs, indicating that the fluorescence of SQDs may be quenched by p-NP through IFE. Therefore, based on the above experimental results, it can be concluded that the fluorescence quenching mechanism of SQDs that can selectively detect p-NP is IFE [52,53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing p-NP in the environmental water sample
ce:section.ce:para: Since the components contained in the actual sample cannot be completely determined, and there were potential unknown interferences, the actual sample analysis is more challenging. In order to prove the feasibility of the synthesized fluorescent SQDs for detecting p-NP in water environments, we selected mineral water, tap water, and Jialing River water samples to conduct multiple parallel experiments, but p-NP was not detected in all water samples. Therefore, the accuracy of the method was further verified by adding p-NP standard solutions of different concentrations. The analysis results in Table S2 show that it has a good recovery rate (90.9%–102.0%) and relative standard deviation (0.33%–1.66%) for the determination of p-NP, indicating that this method can be used as an alternative method for detecting p-NP in actual environmental samples.

In summary, the prepared SQDs have tunable emission from blue to green, excellent stability and excellent aqueous dispersibility. The synthesized SQDs were used as a fluorescent probe to quickly and sensitively detect p-NP. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is the internal filtering effect. By adding p-NP standard solutions of different concentrations in the actual water samples, an excellent recovery rate (90.9%–102.0%) was obtained. This work provides a simple and feasible method for the detection of p-NP in the environment, and at the same time provides a new way for the construction of p-NP fluorescent probes in a rapid detection environment.

Xiaohui Peng: Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Ya Wang: Data curation, Writing - original draft. Zhen Luo: Validation, Writing - review & editing. Baowen Zhang: Methodology, Software. Xiaolin Mei: Methodology. Xiupei Yang: Conceptualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21777130, 41807210), the Natural Science Foundation of Sichuan Province of China (2019YJ0522, 2018JY0184), the Meritocracy Research Funds of China West Normal University (463132), and the Fundamental Research Funds of China West Normal University (416390).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106735.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nutrient digestibility, rumen microbial protein synthesis, and growth performance in sheep consuming rations containing sea buckthorn pomace
